Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to catch up on the online courses, but with eight of them running simultaneously, I often felt overwhelmed before I even started. I'm someone who learns best by doing things hands-on, so sitting and watching videos or reading materials never felt natural to me. I'd usually end up studying in short bursts whenever I could manage my anxiety enough to focus.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on how anxious I was feeling on any given day. Some weeks I'd manage to stick to a schedule, but then something would trigger my anxiety and I'd fall behind. Deadlines definitely pushed me to study more, but by then I was often too stressed to absorb anything properly. I wish I could say I was consistent, but the truth is my mental state dictated my study times more than any planned routine.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a new assignment or a peer reaching out through the support program, and that would prompt me to check. Fear was honestly my main motivator - fear of falling even further behind, fear of failing completely. It wasn't a healthy relationship with the platform.
Q4: There was a brief period early in the semester where logging in after dinner became somewhat routine for me. The peer support program helped a bit because I felt accountable to someone checking in on me. But that routine fell apart when my anxiety got worse around midterms. After that, logging in felt more like a source of dread than a normal habit.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful when I could focus on them - I watched quite a lot of them. I liked being able to pause and replay sections when something didn't click the first time. The discussion boards were occasionally useful because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one struggling. But honestly, nothing replaced actually doing practice problems with my hands, which the LMS didn't really support well.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt too passive for how I learn - I need to be physically doing something to understand concepts. The discussion posts beyond what was required felt like extra pressure I couldn't handle. I also delayed looking at feedback on assignments because I was scared of what it would say, even though my assignment scores were actually decent.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or when it involved actually working through problems rather than just consuming information. Practice exercises where I could get my hands dirty, so to speak, felt valuable. If something was going to take hours of passive reading with no clear outcome, my anxious brain would convince me it wasn't worth the stress.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps to me, honestly. My anxiety made me feel like I should do everything, but my low motivation meant I couldn't. Required activities at least gave me clear boundaries - this is what you must do, period. Optional ones created this horrible guilt spiral where I knew I should do them but couldn't bring myself to, which made me feel even worse about myself.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the course materials and worked through them on paper because that's how I actually learn. I'd print out problem sets and work through them at my desk with a pencil, which felt so much more natural than clicking through screens. I also used some external YouTube tutorials when the course explanations didn't click for me.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened away from the computer. I'd watch a video once online, then spend hours working through the concepts on paper or trying to explain them out loud to myself. My assignment scores were really high because I put in work that the LMS couldn't see - lots of practice problems, lots of rewriting notes by hand. The LMS only saw me download something once, not the three hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: Constantly. I'd download lecture slides or problem sets in one session, then work with them for days offline. My internet is reliable, but staring at screens for too long makes my anxiety worse, so I preferred printed materials. The LMS would show one brief access, but that material might have been my main study resource for an entire week.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. My anxiety spiked to the point where just thinking about logging into the LMS made my heart race. I was juggling eight online courses, and the sheer volume of content across all of them felt crushing. The peer support helped a little, but my resilience just isn't strong - once I started struggling, it was hard to climb back up.
Q13: The workload across eight courses was genuinely unmanageable for someone with my anxiety levels. Confusion about course material made me avoid the LMS because I didn't want to face how lost I was. Fatigue was constant because anxiety disrupts my sleep. When I was exhausted and scared, the LMS became something to avoid rather than a tool to help me.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in and scroll through materials in a daze, not really absorbing anything because my mind was racing with worry. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while I was actually mentally spiraling about other deadlines or life stuff. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was mentally present at all.
Q15: I postponed looking at exam preparation materials because I was terrified of how much I didn't know. The fear of confirming my worst anxieties about failing kept me from engaging with the very things that might have helped me pass. I knew the exam materials were important, but facing them felt like facing proof that I wasn't good enough. My low exam score reflects that avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines created panic-driven bursts of activity. I'd avoid the LMS for days, then cram everything in the last hours before something was due. This actually worked okay for assignments since I scored well on those, but it was terrible for exam preparation which required sustained study. The deadline pressure sometimes helped me overcome my avoidance, but it wasn't healthy or effective for deeper learning.
Q17: I was honestly afraid to look at feedback. When I did force myself to read it, positive feedback gave me temporary relief, but negative feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral for days. The peer support intervention helped because having someone else acknowledge my efforts felt different than just seeing a grade. But I definitely avoided feedback more than I engaged with it.
Q18: The course structure with eight simultaneous online courses was overwhelming for someone like me. Having so many deadlines across different courses made it impossible to develop a steady rhythm with any single one. The structure assumed a level of self-regulation and resilience that I just don't have right now. More scaffolding and fewer simultaneous demands would have helped me enormously.
Q19: The self-paced nature was supposed to be flexible, but for me it meant endless opportunities to procrastinate and avoid. Having clear, smaller milestones might have helped me stay on track. The kinesthetic learning I need wasn't really supported - everything was watching and reading rather than doing. More interactive, hands-on activities would have made engagement feel more natural to me.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me - logging in frequently out of anxiety, clicking through things in a panic, but being too overwhelmed or scared to actually complete and submit work. You might be checking obsessively to see what's due, watching your grades, reading and rereading instructions, but never feeling capable enough to submit something. High activity can mask paralysis.
Q21: That makes sense to me because I know my assignment scores were high even though my overall LMS activity wasn't exceptional. If someone downloads materials efficiently and studies offline, their LMS footprint would be small but their learning could be solid. The platform doesn't capture paper-based studying, external resources, or the quality of offline engagement at all.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and what they click on, but they can't show the quality of attention or emotional state during that time. They miss offline studying entirely, which for kinesthetic learners like me is where real learning happens. They also can't capture fear, avoidance, or the mental energy it takes someone with anxiety just to open the platform in the first place.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting genuine mental effort into learning, not just completing tasks. For me, engagement looks like working through problems by hand until concepts click, even if that doesn't show up anywhere online. It's about being mentally present and invested, which is different from just being logged in or submitting assignments.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much my anxiety shaped every interaction with the course. It can't show the hours I spent studying offline with printed materials, or the fear that kept me from accessing exam prep resources. It doesn't capture that my peer support connection helped me feel less alone even when my performance didn't improve. And it definitely doesn't show that I actually understood the material well enough to score high on assignments - my exam failure was about anxiety and avoidance, not lack of ability.
